sources.title: Grade 10 - Topic 4: Transformations in southern Africa after 1750
The Mfecane: Understanding a Period of Transformation in Southern Africa
The Zulu kingdom and the colony of Natal
History Grade 10 - Topic 4 Essay Questions and Answers

slides.heading: Transformations in Southern Africa after 1750
slides.lead: The Mfecane/Difaqane, the rise of the Zulu kingdom under Shaka, and the reshaping of politics across the region.

image.alt: A stylised map of southern Africa with arrows radiating outward from an eastern coastal zone, representing the movement of people during the Mfecane.

slides.notes: Open by orienting learners in time and place: 'after 1750' means the decades before and during the early 1800s in the interior and eastern coastal belt of what is now South Africa, Lesotho, eSwatini, Mozambique and Zimbabwe. Preview the three threads: (1) a period of upheaval called the Mfecane (Nguni) or Difaqane/Lifaqane (Sotho-Tswana), (2) the rise of a powerful centralised Zulu kingdom, and (3) how neighbouring peoples were displaced or built new states in response. Stress that this is a period historians actively debate.

slides.heading: Southern Africa before the upheaval
slides.lead: By the late 1700s the region was home to many independent African chiefdoms - not empty land waiting to be settled.

blocks.text: Along the fertile eastern coast lived **Nguni-speaking** chiefdoms; on the highveld lived **Sotho-Tswana** communities. Most were mixed farmers who herded cattle and grew sorghum and maize, and were organised into chiefdoms of varying size.

items: Cattle were wealth, status and the basis of political loyalty.
Trade in ivory and cattle linked the interior to the Portuguese port at Delagoa Bay (Maputo).
Growing populations meant more competition for land, water and grazing.

slides.notes: Correct the old myth of an 'empty' interior: this was a settled, productive region with established political systems. Emphasise cattle as central to economy and politics - a chief's power rested on cattle and the followers it could attract. Flag the pressures building up (population growth, competition for grazing, trade rivalries) because these feed directly into the debate about the causes of the Mfecane on the next slides.

slides.heading: What was the Mfecane / Difaqane?
slides.lead: A period of widespread warfare, migration and political change in the early nineteenth century.

blocks.text: **Mfecane** is a Nguni word often translated as 'the crushing' or 'the scattering'; **Difaqane** (or *Lifaqane*) is the Sotho-Tswana term for the same upheaval, usually dated to roughly **1815 to 1840**. Chiefdoms fought, broke apart and re-formed, and large numbers of people were displaced.

items: Small chiefdoms were absorbed into larger, more centralised kingdoms.
Refugees moved across the highveld, disrupting communities far from the coast.
New states such as the Zulu, Ndebele, Sotho, Swazi and Gaza kingdoms took shape.

image.alt: Small scattered circles on the left merging into a few large circles on the right, showing small chiefdoms consolidating into larger kingdoms.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 160' role='img'><rect width='300' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#9aa77f'><circle cx='30' cy='40' r='9'/><circle cx='60' cy='70' r='7'/><circle cx='38' cy='100' r='8'/><circle cx='70' cy='120' r='6'/><circle cx='95' cy='55' r='7'/><circle cx='85' cy='95' r='9'/></g><path d='M120 80 h40' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' fill='none'/><path d='M156 72 l12 8 l-12 8 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='215' cy='55' r='26'/><circle cx='245' cy='110' r='20'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Give learners both names and insist they use them side by side - the two terms reflect the perspectives of different language groups experiencing the same events. Define the process, not just the word: consolidation (many small units becoming a few big ones), migration and state-formation. Warn that dates and death tolls are contested, which sets up the historiography slide later.

slides.heading: Competing explanations for the upheaval
slides.lead: Historians disagree about the causes. A good answer weighs several factors rather than blaming one person.

blocks.heading: Environment
blocks.text: Population growth and a severe drought (remembered as the Madlathule famine) intensified competition for land and grazing.

blocks.heading: Trade
blocks.text: Rivalry to control the ivory and cattle trade through Delagoa Bay encouraged chiefs to build larger, militarised states.

blocks.heading: Politics
blocks.text: Ambitious leaders like Dingiswayo and Shaka centralised power, absorbing weaker neighbours through conquest and alliance.

blocks.heading: Outside pressure
blocks.text: Some historians argue slave-raiding from Delagoa Bay and labour demand from the Cape frontier drove much of the violence.

slides.notes: This is the analytical heart of the topic for FET. Model multi-causal thinking: no single cause is sufficient. Note that older textbooks blamed Shaka's aggression almost alone ('great man' history), while more recent scholarship stresses environment, trade and external raiding. The fourth card previews the Cobbing debate covered later. Encourage learners to rank and connect causes rather than list them.

slides.heading: Dingiswayo and the age-regiments
slides.lead: Before Shaka, Chief Dingiswayo of the Mthethwa reorganised how young men served the state.

blocks.text: **Dingiswayo** built the Mthethwa into a dominant power among the northern Nguni. He is associated with organising young men into **amabutho** - age-regiments that served the chief - which strengthened central authority and military capacity.

items: Amabutho grouped men by age rather than by clan, binding them to the ruler.
Dingiswayo took the young Shaka under his protection and promoted him.
After Dingiswayo was killed by a rival, Shaka absorbed the Mthethwa into a growing Zulu power.

slides.notes: Position Dingiswayo as the bridge figure so learners do not treat Shaka as inventing everything from nothing - continuity and change. The amabutho system is the key institution: by organising loyalty around age-sets serving the ruler, chiefs could override clan divisions and field disciplined forces. Note that details of Dingiswayo's life come partly from later oral tradition and should be treated with some caution.

slides.eyebrow: The rise of the Zulu kingdom
slides.heading: Shaka and Zulu military reform
slides.lead: From about 1816 Shaka kaSenzangakhona turned a small chiefdom into the region's most powerful state.

items: The short stabbing spear (iklwa) for close combat, replacing the throwing spear.
The 'horns of the bull' formation to surround and trap enemies.
A disciplined, full-time army built on the amabutho regiments.
Defeated rivals were absorbed rather than simply raided.
A single Zulu identity was built from many separate chiefdoms.
Power was centralised in the king, backed by the regiments.

image.alt: A diagram of the horns of the bull formation: a central block of figures (the chest) with two curving flanks (the horns) reaching around an enemy position.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 170' role='img'><rect width='300' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='120' y='70' width='60' height='40' rx='4' fill='#2f5233'/><path d='M120 90 q-60 -70 -100 30' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='12' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M180 90 q60 -70 100 30' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='12' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='150' cy='30' r='12' fill='#8a1f1f'/><text x='150' y='96' font-size='11' fill='#f4efe6' text-anchor='middle'>chest</text></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the tactics concretely: the 'chest' pins the enemy while the two 'horns' encircle them and the 'loins' (reserve) wait behind, often facing away so as not to lose heart. Stress that the reforms were as much political as military - Shaka absorbed conquered peoples into a new Zulu nation, which is why the kingdom outlasted him. Handle Shaka carefully: avoid both hero-worship and the old racist caricature of a bloodthirsty tyrant; present him as a skilled, ruthless state-builder within a violent era.

slides.heading: Consolidating the Zulu kingdom
slides.lead: Shaka's state concentrated power, cattle and people under the king.

blocks.text: The Zulu kingdom controlled cattle, land and the labour of the regiments through the king. This centralisation made the state strong, but it also created tensions. In **1828 Shaka was assassinated** by his half-brothers **Dingane** and Mhlangana; Dingane then took the throne.

items: The amabutho could not marry until the king released them, keeping men in service.
Tribute in cattle and grain flowed to the royal capital.
Under Dingane the kingdom soon faced a new threat: the Voortrekkers of the Great Trek.

slides.notes: Show how centralisation was the source of both strength and instability - concentrating power invites rivalry at the top, hence the assassination. Preview the collision with the Great Trek (Retief's killing and the Battle of Blood River in 1838) which learners study more fully under colonial expansion. Keep the focus here on the internal workings of the Zulu state rather than the trekker wars.

slides.heading: Displacement and new kingdoms
slides.lead: Groups fleeing or breaking away from the conflict carried the upheaval far inland and beyond.

blocks.text: As chiefdoms were conquered or chose to leave, leaders founded new states well beyond Zululand. **Mzilikazi**, once one of Shaka's commanders, broke away and led the **Ndebele (Matabele)** onto the highveld and eventually north across the Limpopo into present-day Zimbabwe.

items: Mzilikazi's Ndebele raided and reshaped highveld communities before settling near Bulawayo.
Soshangane founded the Gaza kingdom in what is now southern Mozambique.
Sobhuza I gathered peoples in the north to form the core of the Swazi kingdom.

image.alt: A simple map with labelled arrows showing the Ndebele moving north-west onto the highveld and beyond the Limpopo, Gaza to the north-east, and Swazi to the north.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 170' role='img'><rect width='300' height='170' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M40 30 q90 -6 200 30 q30 40 -10 90 q-100 26 -190 -6 q-20 -50 0 -114 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='210' cy='110' r='7' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='3' fill='none'><path d='M205 108 L120 70'/><path d='M120 70 L90 40'/><path d='M212 106 L250 60'/><path d='M210 103 L215 55'/></g><g fill='#2f5233' font-size='9'><text x='70' y='36'>Ndebele</text><text x='250' y='54'>Gaza</text><text x='196' y='50'>Swazi</text></g></svg>

slides.notes: Use this slide to show scale: the Mfecane/Difaqane was not just a Zulu story but a regional transformation stretching to Zimbabwe and Mozambique. Reinforce that many of these movements produced lasting kingdoms and modern national identities. Be careful to present displaced peoples as active agents rebuilding their societies, not merely as passive victims.

slides.heading: Moshoeshoe and the Basotho kingdom
slides.lead: Not every response was warfare - some leaders built security through diplomacy and shelter.

blocks.text: On the highveld **Moshoeshoe I** gathered refugees and scattered communities and built a new **Basotho (Sotho)** nation. From about **1824** he made his stronghold on the flat-topped mountain of **Thaba Bosiu**, which was easy to defend against raiders.

items: He absorbed and protected people fleeing the Difaqane, growing his following.
He used diplomacy, cattle loans and marriage alliances as well as defence.
The kingdom he founded became the basis of modern Lesotho.

image.alt: A flat-topped mountain stronghold with small dwellings on top, representing Thaba Bosiu.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 160' role='img'><rect width='260' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='260' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M50 120 L70 60 L190 60 L210 120 Z' fill='#7a6a52'/><rect x='70' y='54' width='120' height='8' fill='#8a7458'/><g fill='#5c4a34'><rect x='92' y='40' width='16' height='14' rx='7'/><rect x='120' y='38' width='16' height='16' rx='7'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='16' height='14' rx='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Contrast Moshoeshoe's strategy with the militarised path of Shaka - both were state-builders, but Moshoeshoe relied heavily on diplomacy, refuge and a natural fortress. This gives learners a second model of leadership and shows that consolidation could be defensive as well as aggressive. Link forward to the later Basotho Wars against the Free State and to modern Lesotho.

slides.heading: Key moments, c.1795-1840

blocks.alt: A horizontal timeline of the Mfecane period from the late 1790s to the late 1830s.

events.label: Dingiswayo builds Mthethwa power and the amabutho system
Shaka becomes Zulu chief and begins expansion
Mzilikazi breaks away and leads the Ndebele inland
Moshoeshoe settles at Thaba Bosiu, building the Basotho kingdom
Shaka assassinated; Dingane takes the Zulu throne
Difaqane reshapes the highveld; Great Trek reaches the interior

slides.notes: Use the timeline to consolidate sequence and overlap - note that several processes happened at the same time across different regions, not in a neat line. Remind learners that many of these dates are approximate and reconstructed from oral tradition and later records, which is exactly why historians debate the period. This slide is a good pause point before the historiography discussion.

slides.heading: The Mfecane debate
slides.lead: In the 1980s and 1990s historians questioned the traditional story of the Mfecane itself.

blocks.text: The historian **Julian Cobbing** argued that the idea of a self-contained African 'Mfecane' had been used as an *alibi* - a cover story that blamed African violence while hiding European **slave-raiding** from Delagoa Bay and **labour raiding** on the Cape frontier. Other historians, such as Elizabeth Eldredge, questioned parts of his argument and offered a middle position.
The concept of the Mfecane has been used to explain away the role of outsiders in the violence of the period.
blocks.cite: Paraphrase of the argument associated with Julian Cobbing (1988)

items: Traditional view: internal African causes, centred on Shaka and the Zulu.
Revisionist view: European raiding and labour demand drove much of the disruption.
Most historians now favour a combination of internal and external causes.

slides.notes: This slide develops the FET skill of understanding history as interpretation. Explain 'historiography' (the study of how history is written) and why the same events can be told very differently. Keep it balanced: Cobbing's challenge was important and forced a rethink, but many of his specific claims are disputed, and the mainstream position is a synthesis. [SME] Verify the wording and dating of Cobbing's and Eldredge's arguments and the quoted paraphrase against the prescribed CAPS textbook before use; the quote is illustrative, not a verbatim citation.

slides.heading: How the region was transformed
slides.lead: The upheaval left lasting political, social and demographic changes.

blocks.text: By about 1840 the political map of southern Africa looked very different. A few large, centralised kingdoms had replaced many small chiefdoms, and the movement of people had emptied and repopulated large areas. Some regions of the highveld appeared less densely settled to arriving Voortrekkers - a fact used to justify claims of 'empty land'.

items: Powerful new kingdoms: Zulu, Ndebele, Swazi, Gaza and Basotho.
The amabutho model of a centralised, militarised state spread widely.
The disruption shaped how, and where, later colonial settlement took place.

slides.notes: Draw the threads together around continuity and change. Warn learners explicitly against the 'empty land' myth: areas were disrupted and repopulated, not naturally empty, and the myth was later used to justify dispossession. Note the death-toll figures sometimes quoted for the Mfecane (often cited as one to two million) are highly uncertain and probably exaggerated. [SME] Confirm which consequences and any casualty figures the CAPS curriculum expects learners to reproduce.

slides.heading: What we learned - and questions to answer
slides.lead: The period after 1750 reshaped southern Africa's kingdoms, peoples and borders - and it remains actively debated.

blocks.text: You have traced the **Mfecane/Difaqane**, the rise of the **Zulu kingdom** under **Shaka**, the founding of new states by **Mzilikazi, Sobhuza and Moshoeshoe**, and the historians' **debate** about what caused it all.

blocks.label: Q1
blocks.question: Explain what the terms 'Mfecane' and 'Difaqane' mean, and why both terms are used for the same period.

blocks.label: Q2
blocks.question: Discuss the military and political changes Shaka introduced, and explain how they helped build the Zulu kingdom.

blocks.label: Q3
blocks.question: Historians disagree about the causes of the Mfecane. Compare at least two explanations and give your own supported judgement.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as written work or structured discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS source-based and extended-writing styles. Q1 checks concepts, Q2 checks cause-and-effect analysis of the Zulu state, and Q3 assesses the higher-order skill of weighing competing interpretations. Encourage learners to reference evidence and to acknowledge uncertainty. Remind them this topic links forward to Topic 5, colonial expansion in South Africa after 1750.
